title: Research Operations Sourcebook
description: An operating manual for user research operations, organised as rules, principles, guidance and conduct standards across the 8 pillars of ResearchOps.
creator: ResearchOps Governance Team
rights: Draft sourcebook content. ReOps Community 8-pillar model used with attribution under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International.

attribution.name: ResearchOps Community
attribution.relationship: The 8 pillars of ResearchOps are used as the sourcebook's primary taxonomy.

attribution.name: Research That Scales by Kate Towsey
attribution.relationship: Conceptual benchmark for operating-system language, planning, value framing, maturity and scale. No source text is reproduced.

attribution.name: researchops/consentform
attribution.relationship: Conceptual benchmark for simplifying privacy and consent statements through structured questions and generated participant-facing wording. No source text is reproduced.

rule.label: Rule
rule.definition: A mandatory operating requirement. Teams must either comply or record an approved exception.

principle.label: Principle
principle.definition: A durable judgement standard used to interpret the sourcebook when a specific rule does not cover the situation.

guidance.label: Guidance
guidance.definition: Recommended practice that helps teams meet rules and principles consistently.

conduct.label: Conduct
conduct.definition: A behavioural expectation for people planning, running, supporting or using research.

clauseModel.numbering: Sections use n.0.0 and clauses use n.n.n, following the FCA handbook pattern of a stable identifier plus a separate content-type marker.

operatingModel.valueFrame: Each pillar must show how it reduces risk, improves research quality, protects participants, saves operational effort or increases the value of research evidence.

positioning.title: Formal codification layer
positioning.summary: The sourcebook is the formal layer on top of existing ReOps community assets. The community provides the pillars, templates, maturity approaches, repository guidance, toolbox thinking and capability material. The sourcebook turns that material into an operating manual with stable provisions and reviewable obligations.
sourcebookContribution: rule, principle, guidance and conduct types

adoptionMethod.title: Pace Layers Matrix
adoptionMethod.summary: Use pace-layer thinking to decide how to adopt the sourcebook. Start with the slower layers that set direction and constraints, then use faster layers to learn, improve and feed evidence back into the operating system.
steps: Map the current research operations pressure points against the 8 pillars.
Decide which pace layer each pressure point belongs to: strategic intent, governance, infrastructure, service workflow or day-to-day practice.
Prioritise slow-layer work when teams lack direction, decision rights or safe constraints.
Prioritise fast-layer work when teams need quick learning, visible relief or evidence for a larger operating change.
Review the matrix every quarter so learning from fast work updates slower rules, standards and investment decisions.

layers.adoptionQuestion: What must research operations make possible for the organisation, and what risk must it reduce?
layers.sourcebookUse: Adopt organisational context and governance clauses first when research lacks sponsorship, authority, funding or ethical boundaries.

layers.adoptionQuestion: Which decisions need clearer rules before teams can move faster with confidence?
layers.sourcebookUse: Use governance and scope clauses to set intake, triage, approval, exception and escalation routes.

layers.adoptionQuestion: Which shared systems need to become stable before research can be reused or scaled?
layers.sourcebookUse: Use infrastructure and data clauses to prioritise tooling, repository, access, retention, taxonomy and integration work.

layers.adoptionQuestion: Where are participants, researchers or delivery teams feeling avoidable friction or risk?
layers.sourcebookUse: Use recruitment and environment clauses to improve sourcing, consent, scheduling, participant care, accessibility, fieldwork and remote settings.

layers.adoptionQuestion: What can teams practise, learn or standardise now while slower operating decisions mature?
layers.sourcebookUse: Use people and scope clauses to shape coaching, community standards, self-service support, methods guidance and visible operating learning.

operatingPatterns.title: Consent and privacy statement generator
operatingPatterns.summary: Use structured questions to produce clear participant-facing consent and privacy statements, so researchers spend less time translating governance requirements and participants receive better explanations.
operatingPatterns.operatingIntent: Make consent and privacy review faster without weakening human accountability, legal review or participant agency.
minimumQuestions: What is the purpose of the research and what will participants be asked to do?
What personal information, recordings or screen/device data will be collected?
Who can access the material and will anything be shared outside the immediate study team?
Where will the material be stored, how long will it be retained and how can withdrawal or deletion be handled?
Who can participants contact about the research, privacy or data rights?
governanceUse: Use generated wording as a draft that researchers and governance reviewers can check, not as automatic approval.
Keep the question set, generated statement, reviewer decision and participant-facing copy in the study record.
Review the pattern whenever privacy law, organisational policy, tooling or research methods change.

qualityGates.title: 8-pillar completeness
qualityGates.rule: The sourcebook must contain exactly the 8 ResearchOps pillars, each with a code, route, definition, owner, operating question, sections and clauses.

qualityGates.title: Clause model integrity
qualityGates.rule: Every clause must have a stable identifier, content type, status, effective date, title, text, applies-to audience and evidence expectation.

qualityGates.title: Metadata continuity
qualityGates.rule: Sourcebook pages must render Dublin Core, RDFa and SKOS metadata for the sourcebook, pillar, section and clause concepts.

qualityGates.title: Handbook readability
qualityGates.rule: Rules, principles, guidance and conduct standards must be distinguishable in navigation, page headings and clause badges.

qualityGates.title: REC-ADMN reference quality
qualityGates.rule: REC-ADMN must include the full operating cycle for recruitment: operating model, sourcing, consent, privacy, incentives, scheduling, participant care, reporting and learning.

pillars.title: Environment
pillars.operatingQuestion: Where and under what conditions can research happen safely, inclusively and effectively?
pillars.definition: The Environment pillar covers research spaces, fieldwork conditions, remote research settings, accessibility, researcher safety and participant comfort.
pillars.valueFrame: Good environments reduce session risk, improve participant trust and make high-quality research repeatable across locations and channels.

sections.title: Environment operating model
sections.definition: How research settings, channels, safety, inclusion and readiness are governed across the research lifecycle.

clauses.title: The research environment is part of the method
clauses.text: Research environments should be chosen, prepared and reviewed as part of research quality, participant care and researcher safety rather than treated as logistics.

clauses.title: Assess the setting before research starts
clauses.text: Every study must assess whether the physical, remote or field setting supports the method, participant needs, confidentiality, safeguarding and researcher safety before sessions begin.

clauses.title: Choose the lowest-friction setting that still protects quality
clauses.text: Use remote, lab, office or field settings according to the research question, participant context, privacy risk and access needs rather than team convenience alone.

clauses.title: Do not make people absorb environmental risk
clauses.text: People planning research must not expect participants, researchers or observers to compensate for unsafe, inaccessible, noisy, exposed or poorly prepared settings.

sections.title: Accessibility, inclusion and reasonable adjustments
sections.definition: How research settings support access needs, inclusive participation and reasonable adjustments.

clauses.title: Offer and record environment adjustments
clauses.text: Participants must be offered a clear route to request environment adjustments before research, and agreed adjustments must be recorded and prepared before the session.

clauses.title: Inclusive settings improve evidence quality
clauses.text: Research settings should reduce avoidable barriers so participants can take part in ways that reflect their real experience, not the constraints of the research setup.

clauses.title: Check accessibility across the whole journey
clauses.text: Consider travel, arrival, waiting, sign-in, digital access, assistive technology, breaks, captions, interpreters, support people and follow-up rather than only the interview room or video call.

sections.title: Physical spaces and controlled research settings
sections.definition: How labs, offices, usability rooms and other controlled spaces are prepared for research.

clauses.title: Prepare the physical space before participants arrive
clauses.text: Physical research spaces must be checked for privacy, accessibility, comfort, recording setup, observer arrangements, emergency routes and participant arrival before participants arrive.

clauses.title: Make observers visible in the consent model
clauses.text: If observers are present physically or remotely, explain who they are, why they are there, what they can see or hear and how participants can object.

clauses.title: Protect participant comfort in controlled spaces
clauses.text: People supporting research must make it easy for participants to pause, ask for adjustments, decline recording or leave without pressure.

sections.title: Remote and digital research settings
sections.definition: How remote research environments, digital access and recording conditions are managed.

clauses.title: Check remote readiness before the session
clauses.text: Remote research must confirm participant access, device needs, platform familiarity, privacy conditions, recording consent, backup contact route and support for technical issues before the session.

clauses.title: Remote research should not lower care standards
clauses.text: Remote sessions should provide the same clarity, consent, access, confidentiality and participant care as physical sessions, adapted to the channel.

clauses.title: Have a fallback when technology fails
clauses.text: For remote research, prepare a fallback route for joining, contacting the participant, rescheduling, preserving consent choices and recording any data quality limitations.

sections.title: Fieldwork, community and high-variability settings
sections.definition: How research outside controlled spaces is planned, supported and reviewed.

clauses.title: Complete fieldwork risk planning
clauses.text: Fieldwork and community research must have a risk plan covering location, travel, lone working, safeguarding, confidentiality, local permission, participant distress and escalation routes.

clauses.title: Work with local context rather than around it
clauses.text: When research happens in a participant's home, workplace, community setting or public place, adapt the plan to local norms, power dynamics, privacy and safety conditions.

clauses.title: Do not trade safety for access
clauses.text: People planning fieldwork must not accept unsafe conditions, weak safeguarding or unclear permission simply because a setting gives access to a hard-to-reach group.

sections.title: Researcher safety, wellbeing and session support
sections.definition: How the environment protects researchers, facilitators, note-takers and support roles.

clauses.title: Plan safety for people running research
clauses.text: Research plans must identify safety, emotional load, lone working, observer conduct, escalation and debrief needs for people running or supporting sessions.

clauses.title: Match support to session risk
clauses.text: Use co-facilitation, observer limits, check-ins, safeguarding support or post-session debriefs where topic sensitivity, location, participant distress or researcher experience increases risk.

clauses.title: A safe environment protects research quality
clauses.text: Researchers who feel safe and supported are better able to listen, adapt, notice risk and protect participants during research.

sections.title: Environment review and operating learning
sections.definition: How environment issues, participant feedback and session learning improve future research settings.

clauses.title: Record material environment issues
clauses.text: Material issues with access, safety, privacy, technology, comfort, observer behaviour or setting suitability must be recorded and reviewed after the research activity.

clauses.title: Turn repeated environment issues into standards
clauses.text: When the same setting problems recur, update checklists, guidance, templates, supplier choices or support models rather than relying on individual memory.

clauses.title: Good environments make research safer, fairer and easier to repeat
clauses.text: A mature environment system should reduce avoidable risk, improve inclusion, strengthen participant trust and make high-quality research repeatable across channels.

glossary.definition: The physical, remote, digital, fieldwork or community setting in which research is planned, run, observed or supported.
A change to the setting, channel, timing, support or tools that helps a participant or researcher take part safely and effectively.
A research setting outside a controlled research space, including homes, workplaces, public places and community locations.
A material problem with access, safety, privacy, comfort, technology or setting suitability that affects research quality or care.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded ENVIRO into an end-to-end environment lifecycle covering operating model, accessibility, controlled spaces, remote research, fieldwork, researcher safety and operating learning.

pillars.title: Scope
pillars.operatingQuestion: What research should happen, who may do it and what level of support or review does it need?
pillars.definition: The Scope pillar defines the boundaries of research work, request intake, prioritisation, risk classification, method choice and support model.
pillars.valueFrame: Clear scope directs limited research capacity towards the highest-value questions and stops low-quality or unsafe research from entering delivery unnoticed.

sections.title: Scope operating model
sections.definition: How the organisation defines what counts as research work, who can do it and what level of operating support it needs.

clauses.title: Research scope is an operating decision
clauses.text: Research scope should connect organisational priorities, decision needs, participant risk, available capability and the operating support needed to run the work well.

clauses.title: Define the boundary between research and adjacent activity
clauses.text: The practice must define which activities are governed as user research, which are adjacent discovery or engagement activities and which require research leadership review before proceeding.

clauses.title: Use scope to choose the operating model
clauses.text: Scope decisions should identify whether work is best supported through self-service, coaching, full-service research, specialist governance or a mixed model.

clauses.title: Do not relabel weak research to avoid governance
clauses.text: Teams must not describe research as feedback, validation, engagement or discovery to avoid required review, participant protections or evidence standards.

sections.title: Research intake and problem framing
sections.definition: How research demand enters the system and is shaped into a clear decision need.

clauses.title: Record a research rationale before work starts
clauses.text: Every research activity must have a recorded problem, decision need, user group, method rationale and expected use of findings before recruitment or fieldwork starts.

clauses.title: Name the decision the research will inform
clauses.text: Research requests must state the decision, risk, policy question, service assumption or design choice that evidence is expected to inform.

clauses.title: Separate symptoms from researchable questions
clauses.text: When an intake request describes a solution, complaint or vague learning goal, reframe it into the decision, user group, uncertainty and evidence need before accepting it as research work.

sections.title: Prioritisation and capacity
sections.definition: How research effort is matched to strategy, risk, value and available capability.

clauses.title: Research capacity is a strategic resource
clauses.text: Research effort should be directed to decisions where user evidence can reduce material risk, improve outcomes or change what the organisation does.

clauses.title: Prioritise demand using visible criteria
clauses.text: Research demand must be prioritised using recorded criteria such as user risk, service impact, legal or policy dependency, learning value, urgency, effort and strategic fit.

clauses.title: Make trade-offs explicit when deprioritising work
clauses.text: When research is deferred or declined, record the reason, the risk accepted, the alternative evidence available and the point at which the decision should be revisited.

sections.title: Risk, review and support levels
sections.definition: How the scope of work determines governance, ResearchOps support and delivery controls.

clauses.title: Classify support level before delivery starts
clauses.text: A study must be classified for support level before delivery starts, using risk, method complexity, participant sensitivity, capability and operational dependency.

clauses.title: Use a hybrid support model
clauses.text: Provide self-service templates for low-risk routine work and full-service ResearchOps support for high-risk, high-priority or specialist studies.

clauses.title: Escalate when scope grows beyond approval
clauses.text: People running research must pause and recheck scope when participants, topics, methods, data collection, incentives or intended use expand beyond the approved plan.

sections.title: Method boundaries and evidence standards
sections.definition: How method choice, evidence quality and research claims stay within scope.

clauses.title: Match method to decision risk
clauses.text: The selected method must be proportionate to the decision risk and capable of producing evidence strong enough for the intended use.

clauses.title: Scope should protect evidence from overreach
clauses.text: Research claims should stay within the population, context, method, sample and confidence level covered by the study.

clauses.title: Use method playbooks as guardrails, not scripts
clauses.text: Method playbooks should help teams choose and run suitable approaches while still requiring researchers to adapt responsibly to context and risk.

sections.title: Self-service, coaching and non-researcher activity
sections.definition: How low-risk research-adjacent work is enabled without weakening standards.

clauses.title: Limit self-service research to approved low-risk patterns
clauses.text: Self-service research or research-adjacent activity must stay within approved methods, participant groups, data handling rules and review thresholds.

clauses.title: Ask for help before quality or safety is compromised
clauses.text: People using self-service patterns must seek research or governance support when the work becomes sensitive, ambiguous, high-impact or beyond their competence.

clauses.title: Use coaching to grow capability without hiding risk
clauses.text: Coaching should help teams frame questions, choose safe methods and understand limits, while keeping accountability for high-risk research with qualified researchers.

sections.title: Roadmap, portfolio and scope learning
sections.definition: How scope decisions are reviewed across the portfolio and used to improve the research operating system.

clauses.title: Maintain a visible research backlog or roadmap
clauses.text: Priority research demand, accepted work, deferred work and known evidence gaps must be visible in a backlog or roadmap that research and delivery leaders can review.

clauses.title: Review demand patterns as operating-system evidence
clauses.text: Repeated requests, unclear scope, rejected studies, unsupported self-service work and recurring method gaps should feed operating improvements.

clauses.title: Good scope increases focus and trust
clauses.text: A mature scope system should help teams ask better questions, use research capacity deliberately and understand why some work proceeds, changes or stops.

glossary.definition: The agreed boundary of a research activity, including question, participants, method, intended use, evidence standard and support level.
The decision, risk or uncertainty that research evidence is expected to inform.
The degree of ResearchOps, research leadership or governance involvement needed for a piece of work.
An approved low-risk approach that enables non-researchers or teams to collect limited evidence within defined guardrails.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded SCOPE into an end-to-end scope lifecycle covering operating model, intake, prioritisation, support levels, method boundaries, self-service and portfolio learning.

pillars.title: People
pillars.operatingQuestion: What roles, skills, communities and support are needed for responsible research practice?
pillars.definition: The People pillar covers research roles, capability, community practice, mentoring, wellbeing, collaboration and professional standards.
pillars.valueFrame: Strong people systems protect research quality while making research easier to learn, support and sustain.

sections.title: People operating model
sections.definition: How research roles, accountabilities and community support are designed as part of the research operating system.

clauses.title: Research is a team practice with accountable roles
clauses.text: Research may involve many disciplines, but accountability for method quality, ethics and participant safety must be explicit.

clauses.title: Define research roles and accountabilities
clauses.text: The practice must define the responsibilities, decision rights and escalation routes for researchers, ResearchOps, research leaders, observers, coaches and non-researchers involved in research.

clauses.title: Design support around real operating load
clauses.text: People support should reflect the volume, complexity and risk of research work rather than assuming informal goodwill can absorb coordination, review, coaching and community labour.

clauses.title: Respect invisible operations work
clauses.text: Teams must recognise research coordination, review, coaching, knowledge sharing and participant-care work as real labour that needs time, ownership and credit.

sections.title: Capability, competence and permission
sections.definition: How people are assessed, supported and authorised to conduct different kinds of research activity.

clauses.title: Match research tasks to competence
clauses.text: People must only lead research activities that are appropriate to their assessed competence, supervision and the risk level of the study.

clauses.title: Record permission for non-researcher activity
clauses.text: Non-researchers may only run research or research-adjacent activity within approved scope, method, participant and data-handling boundaries.

clauses.title: Use capability levels to target support
clauses.text: Capability frameworks should guide coaching, review and supervision needs rather than become a performance label detached from practical research risk.

sections.title: Community of practice and professional standards
sections.definition: How the research community learns, shares standards and improves practice without relying on performative visibility.

clauses.title: Maintain a community charter
clauses.text: A research community of practice must have a visible charter that defines purpose, membership, contribution norms, decision rights and how standards are updated.

clauses.title: Make research work visible without making people performative
clauses.text: Community activity should share learning, support practice and create trust without rewarding visible performance over careful research work.

clauses.title: Standards should travel through practice
clauses.text: Research standards should be reinforced through critique, examples, coaching, peer review and reusable artefacts, not only stored in documents.

sections.title: Coaching, mentoring and supervision
sections.definition: How people receive the right level of support before, during and after research work.

clauses.title: Assign supervision for higher-risk research
clauses.text: Researchers or non-researchers leading higher-risk or unfamiliar methods must have a named supervisor, mentor or reviewer before research starts.

clauses.title: Separate coaching from approval
clauses.text: Where possible, distinguish coaching that helps people improve from formal approval decisions, so learners can expose uncertainty without hiding risk.

clauses.title: Give feedback that improves the work
clauses.text: Feedback on research practice must be specific, evidence-based and oriented towards better research decisions, participant care and professional growth.

sections.title: Research wellbeing and psychological safety
sections.definition: How the operating system protects people doing research from avoidable harm, overload and unsupported exposure to difficult work.

clauses.title: Researcher wellbeing is part of research quality
clauses.text: Research quality depends on people having enough support, time, psychological safety and recovery space to make careful ethical judgements.

clauses.title: Assess researcher risk for difficult work
clauses.text: Research involving trauma, distressing topics, safeguarding, hostility, field risk or intense emotional labour must include a researcher wellbeing and support plan.

clauses.title: Do not normalise overload as commitment
clauses.text: Teams must not treat unsustainable research load, repeated emotional exposure or unpaid community labour as evidence of professionalism.

sections.title: Collaboration, observers and communication
sections.definition: How people outside the research role engage with research responsibly.

clauses.title: Brief observers before research sessions
clauses.text: Observers must be briefed on participant respect, confidentiality, note-taking boundaries, communication rules and how to raise concerns before attending research.

clauses.title: Use sharebacks to connect learning to decisions
clauses.text: Research communication should help teams understand evidence, limitations and decisions, not only broadcast interesting findings.

clauses.title: Keep participant respect visible to the whole team
clauses.text: Everyone who observes, discusses or uses research must protect participant dignity, confidentiality and context when turning evidence into decisions.

sections.title: Practice learning and capability planning
sections.definition: How the profession learns from delivery, plans capability and improves the operating system over time.

clauses.title: Review capability gaps on a fixed cycle
clauses.text: Research leadership must review capability gaps, supervision needs, community health and operating load on a fixed cycle and record improvement actions.

clauses.title: Turn repeated support requests into system improvements
clauses.text: Repeated coaching needs, unclear roles, observer issues or community questions should trigger improvements to templates, training, guidance or operating policy.

clauses.title: Good people systems increase research quality and resilience
clauses.text: A mature people system should help research practice stay ethical, skilled, inclusive and sustainable as demand, risk and organisational change increase.

glossary.definition: The practical ability to plan, run, support, review or use research at a level appropriate to risk and context.
A group that helps people share research standards, learning, examples and support across teams.
Named support and oversight for people doing research work that is unfamiliar, higher risk or beyond their current independent competence.
The conditions, support and workload controls that help people doing research make careful judgements without avoidable harm.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded PEOP-COMM into an end-to-end people lifecycle covering operating model, competence, community, coaching, wellbeing, collaboration and capability planning.

pillars.title: Organisational context
pillars.operatingQuestion: How does research fit the organisation's strategy, governance, funding, decision-making and delivery rhythm?
pillars.definition: The Organisational context pillar connects research operations to strategy, funding, decision rights, portfolio planning, stakeholder management and operating constraints.
pillars.valueFrame: ResearchOps creates value when operational systems are connected to the organisation's priorities and decisions.

sections.title: Strategy and value connection
sections.definition: How ResearchOps work is tied to organisational priorities, decision needs and value creation.

clauses.title: Operations should make strategy executable
clauses.text: ResearchOps work should translate strategic priorities into repeatable systems, not only provide administrative support after decisions have been made.

clauses.title: Connect priority research to a strategic objective
clauses.text: Research classed as priority work must record the organisational objective, service risk, policy question or decision it is expected to inform.

clauses.title: Frame value in terms the organisation recognises
clauses.text: Explain ResearchOps value through risk reduction, decision quality, participant protection, delivery efficiency, evidence reuse and strategic learning.

clauses.title: Do not promise certainty from research
clauses.text: People representing research must communicate evidence, confidence and limits honestly when connecting research to organisational goals.

sections.title: Research operating model
sections.definition: How the research practice is structured to create value and interoperate with delivery, policy and organisational governance.

clauses.title: Document the research operating model
clauses.text: The research practice must document how research is requested, prioritised, staffed, governed, funded, delivered, stored, shared and improved.

clauses.title: Operating models should match organisational reality
clauses.text: The research operating model should reflect actual decision routes, power structures, constraints, funding cycles and delivery rhythms rather than an idealised process.

clauses.title: Use planning matrices to expose operating trade-offs
clauses.text: Map each pillar against maturity, effort, risk and value so the team can choose what to standardise, operate, specialise or defer.

sections.title: Funding, capacity and resource planning
sections.definition: How the organisation funds, staffs and sustains research operations.

clauses.title: Plan capacity against demand and risk
clauses.text: Research leadership must review demand, risk, skill availability, operational load and funding constraints before committing to priority research work.

clauses.title: Make the cost of unsupported operations visible
clauses.text: Track repeated manual work, delays, rework, tooling gaps and hidden coordination effort so investment decisions can compare cost, risk and value.

clauses.title: Do not fund research by relying on unsustainable goodwill
clauses.text: Leaders must not rely on unpaid community labour, hidden overtime or informal coordination as a substitute for explicit resourcing.

sections.title: Decision rights and governance integration
sections.definition: How research evidence enters organisational decision-making and governance forums.

clauses.title: Show the decision path for priority research
clauses.text: Research classed as priority work must record the decision owner, decision forum, expected timing, evidence need and how findings will be used.

clauses.title: Evidence should meet decisions where they happen
clauses.text: Research evidence should be timed, shaped and governed so it can influence real decision routes, not only be shared after choices are effectively made.

clauses.title: Integrate research checkpoints with delivery workflows
clauses.text: Use delivery, policy or service governance checkpoints to surface research risks, evidence gaps and unresolved assumptions before decisions harden.

sections.title: Stakeholder alignment and organisational trust
sections.definition: How research builds and maintains trust with people who request, fund, approve or use research.

clauses.title: Map stakeholders for priority operating changes
clauses.text: Material changes to research operations must identify affected stakeholders, decision makers, contributors, risks and communication needs.

clauses.title: Build trust by making operating decisions explainable
clauses.text: When ResearchOps changes routes, rules, tools or support levels, explain the reason, trade-offs, expected benefit and how feedback will be handled.

clauses.title: Do not use stakeholder pressure to bypass standards
clauses.text: People must not use seniority, urgency or delivery pressure to bypass research governance, participant protections or evidence standards.

sections.title: Portfolio rhythm and organisational learning
sections.definition: How research operations works across teams, portfolios and planning cycles.

clauses.title: Review research demand at portfolio level
clauses.text: Research leadership must review demand, evidence gaps, repeated risks and cross-team dependencies at portfolio level on a fixed rhythm.

clauses.title: Turn cross-team patterns into operating improvements
clauses.text: Repeated evidence gaps, duplicated studies, tooling friction or stakeholder confusion should feed the ResearchOps improvement backlog.

clauses.title: Organisational learning should compound
clauses.text: Research operations should help learning travel across teams and over time, while preserving context, limitations and participant protections.

sections.title: Value measurement and operating review
sections.definition: How the organisation reviews whether ResearchOps is creating useful, sustainable value.

clauses.title: Review operating value on a fixed cycle
clauses.text: ResearchOps must review service value, risk reduction, quality indicators, cost drivers, unresolved constraints and operating improvements on a fixed cycle.

clauses.title: Use mixed measures of value
clauses.text: Combine quantitative indicators such as cycle time and reuse with qualitative evidence of decision quality, trust, participant protection and researcher effort.

clauses.title: Good organisational context makes research usable
clauses.text: A mature organisational context should help research influence real decisions, receive sustainable investment and strengthen trust in how the organisation learns.

glossary.definition: The way research people, processes, governance, tools, funding and decision routes work together to create value.
The explicit ownership of who can make, approve, defer or escalate decisions informed by research.
The repeated planning and review cycle that connects research demand, evidence gaps and operating improvements across teams.
The value ResearchOps creates by reducing risk, improving decision quality, protecting participants, saving effort and making evidence reusable.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded ORG-CONT into an end-to-end organisational context lifecycle covering strategy, operating model, funding, decision rights, stakeholder trust, portfolio rhythm and value review.

pillars.title: Recruitment and administration
pillars.operatingQuestion: How are participants sourced, invited, consented, supported, paid, tracked and protected throughout the research lifecycle?
pillars.definition: The Recruitment and administration pillar governs the full participant operations system, including sourcing, screening, scheduling, consent, privacy, incentives, participant records, communications, reporting and learning.
pillars.valueFrame: Recruitment operations should reduce delivery friction while protecting participants, improving sample quality and making the real cost and value of participant access visible.

sections.title: Recruitment operating model
sections.definition: How teams decide whether recruitment is self-service, full-service or hybrid.

clauses.title: Participant access is part of the research operating system
clauses.text: Recruitment must be designed as a system of sourcing, screening, consent, records, scheduling, incentives and learning, not treated as isolated administration.

clauses.title: Classify recruitment support before outreach starts
clauses.text: A study must be classified as self-service, full-service or hybrid before any participant outreach, with the decision based on risk, audience sensitivity, access needs, timescale and sample complexity.

clauses.title: Reserve full-service recruitment for material risk or specialist access
clauses.text: Use full-service recruitment where participant harm, safeguarding, legal sensitivity, hard-to-reach access, high-profile stakeholders or complex incentives require specialist coordination.

clauses.title: Do not make participants carry operational confusion
clauses.text: People coordinating recruitment must give participants clear information, timely updates and a single route for questions, changes or withdrawal.

sections.title: Participant sourcing and screening
sections.definition: How participant sources, sample plans and screeners are selected and reviewed.

clauses.title: Approve the sample rationale
clauses.text: The study lead must document and review the sample rationale, inclusion and exclusion criteria, source channels and known bias risks before screening begins.

clauses.title: Use more than one source where bias would damage the decision
clauses.text: Where a single panel, agency, staff list or community route would distort findings, use mixed sourcing and record the trade-offs.

clauses.title: Screen without extracting unnecessary personal information
clauses.text: Screening questions must be relevant, proportionate and written so participants can understand why information is being requested.

sections.title: Consent, privacy and participant records
sections.definition: How consent, privacy notices and participant records are created, stored, amended and retired.

clauses.title: Record informed consent before research participation
clauses.text: Participants must receive clear study information and give recorded informed consent before taking part, including consent choices for recording, quotation and future contact.

clauses.title: Separate participant identity from research evidence
clauses.text: Participant contact details and direct identifiers must be stored separately from notes, recordings, transcripts and findings unless an approved exception is recorded.

clauses.title: Treat panels as living data products
clauses.text: Participant panels should have named ownership, consent refresh points, retention rules, suppression lists, audit trails and a routine process for removing stale or unsuitable records.

sections.title: Incentives and participant care
sections.definition: How incentives, reimbursement, accessibility support and participant wellbeing are handled.

clauses.title: Incentives should recognise effort without creating pressure
clauses.text: Incentives and reimbursements should be fair, timely and proportionate to participant effort, while avoiding undue influence or exclusion.

clauses.title: Record incentive decisions and payment status
clauses.text: The team must record the incentive offered, approval basis, fulfilment route, payment status and unresolved payment issues for each participant who is due an incentive.

clauses.title: Respect withdrawal and non-attendance
clauses.text: Teams must handle withdrawal, late cancellation and non-attendance without blame, while recording operational impact so future planning improves.

sections.title: Scheduling and communications
sections.definition: How participants, researchers and observers are kept aligned before, during and after sessions.

clauses.title: Confirm session logistics in writing
clauses.text: Participants must receive written confirmation of session time, channel or location, access support, preparation needs, incentive terms, withdrawal route and contact details.

clauses.title: Automate routine reminders after the wording is safe
clauses.text: Use scheduling and reminder automation for repeatable logistics, but review message content for plain language, privacy, access needs and the ability to reply to a person.

clauses.title: Keep observers behind the participant relationship
clauses.text: Observers must not contact participants directly unless the study plan assigns that responsibility and participants have been told.

sections.title: Reporting and operational learning
sections.definition: How recruitment performance, participant experience and operating risks are reviewed.

clauses.title: Review recruitment performance after fieldwork
clauses.text: The study record must include planned sample, achieved sample, source effectiveness, no-show rate, incentive issues, access needs met, unresolved risks and recommendations for future recruitment.

clauses.title: Turn recruitment learning into reusable operations
clauses.text: Where the same recruitment issue appears across studies, treat it as an operating-system improvement candidate rather than a one-off delivery problem.

clauses.title: Measure recruitment by quality and care, not only speed
clauses.text: Recruitment performance should balance time, cost, sample fit, representation, participant experience, researcher effort and risk reduction.

glossary.definition: The decision about whether recruitment is self-service, full-service or hybrid.
The documented reason a participant group, source and screening approach are appropriate for the research question.
The operational record used to manage contact, consent, scheduling, incentive and participation status.

changeHistory.summary: Converted REC-ADMN into the reference-quality clause model with operating model, recruitment, consent, incentives, scheduling and learning sections.

pillars.title: Data and knowledge management
pillars.operatingQuestion: How is research evidence stored, protected, retrieved, reused and connected to decisions?
pillars.definition: The Data and knowledge management pillar covers evidence capture, repositories, taxonomies, metadata, data retention, access control and knowledge reuse.
pillars.valueFrame: Research knowledge compounds when evidence remains findable, trustworthy, governed and connected to decisions after individual projects end.

sections.title: Data and knowledge operating model
sections.definition: How research evidence, participant data, repository ownership and knowledge reuse are governed as an operating system.

clauses.title: Research knowledge is an operational asset
clauses.text: Research evidence should be managed as a shared operating asset with clear ownership, stewardship, access rules, retention expectations and routes back into decisions.

clauses.title: Assign an owner for each research knowledge system
clauses.text: Every research repository, evidence library, archive or knowledge base must have a named owner accountable for access, metadata quality, retention, review cadence and issue escalation.

clauses.title: Separate raw data, analysis material and reusable knowledge
clauses.text: Design the knowledge system so raw participant material, working analysis and decision-ready insights have different storage, access, retention and reuse rules.

clauses.title: Do not treat evidence as team property
clauses.text: People working with research evidence must respect participant conditions, organisational stewardship and future decision needs rather than storing knowledge only in personal files or local team spaces.

sections.title: Evidence capture and repository entry
sections.definition: How research material is captured, described and entered into approved knowledge systems.

clauses.title: Create a repository entry for completed research
clauses.text: Completed research must have a repository entry that records the research question, method, audience, sample, date, owner, consent constraints, artefacts, findings and decision link.

clauses.title: Evidence must keep its context
clauses.text: Research findings and recommendations should retain enough method, sample, limitation and consent context for later users to judge whether the evidence can support a new decision.

clauses.title: Capture the minimum durable record
clauses.text: Where raw material cannot be retained, create a durable summary that preserves the decision need, key evidence, limitations, consent boundaries and route to the study owner.

sections.title: Metadata, taxonomy and findability
sections.definition: How evidence is named, tagged, described and made findable without losing meaning.

clauses.title: Use the approved research taxonomy
clauses.text: Repository entries must use the approved taxonomy for service area, user group, method, risk, artefact type and decision theme unless an exception is recorded.

clauses.title: Tag for decisions, not only projects
clauses.text: Add decision themes, policy areas, service stages and known assumptions so evidence can be discovered when future work cuts across previous project boundaries.

clauses.title: Do not hide uncertainty in labels
clauses.text: People describing evidence must not use overconfident titles, tags or summaries that make weak, old, narrow or contested findings look stronger than they are.

sections.title: Access, privacy and permission
sections.definition: How access to research material is granted, reviewed and constrained by consent, privacy and sensitivity.

clauses.title: Review consent and sensitivity before access is widened
clauses.text: Before research material is reused outside the original study team, the repository owner must check consent, confidentiality, sensitivity, retention limits and whether a lower-risk summary can meet the need.

clauses.title: Access should be proportionate to the evidence need
clauses.text: People should receive the least sensitive form of evidence that can answer their legitimate decision need, with raw participant material treated as exceptional access.

clauses.title: Use summaries for most cross-team reuse
clauses.text: Use de-identified summaries, insight records or evidence packs for most reuse requests, reserving transcripts, recordings and participant-level records for approved specialist review.

sections.title: Retention, disposal and auditability
sections.definition: How evidence is retained, reviewed, archived or disposed of in line with consent and operating value.

clauses.title: Apply retention rules to research material
clauses.text: Research material must have a retention rule that states what is kept, where it is kept, when it is reviewed, when it is deleted or archived and who approves exceptions.

clauses.title: Separate disposal of raw data from retention of learning
clauses.text: When participant-level data reaches its disposal point, retain only the approved level of de-identified learning, decision trace and operating metadata needed for future reuse.

clauses.title: Do not keep participant material because deletion is inconvenient
clauses.text: People managing research material must not retain recordings, transcripts, contact details or sensitive records beyond approved retention limits for convenience or personal backup.

sections.title: Reuse, synthesis and decision trace
sections.definition: How existing research knowledge is reused, synthesised and connected to organisational decisions.

clauses.title: Check existing evidence before commissioning new research
clauses.text: Before commissioning or approving new research, teams must check whether existing evidence can answer the decision need or narrow the research scope.

clauses.title: Reuse should preserve evidence limits
clauses.text: Existing evidence should be reused with its original method, sample, date, context, confidence and consent limits visible to the new decision maker.

clauses.title: Create synthesis when patterns recur
clauses.text: When multiple studies show a recurring pattern, create a synthesis record that states the pattern, source studies, confidence level, contradictions and current decision relevance.

sections.title: Repository health and knowledge learning
sections.definition: How the knowledge system is reviewed, improved and measured for operating value.

clauses.title: Review repository health on a fixed cycle
clauses.text: Repository owners must review repository health on a fixed cycle, including stale entries, missing metadata, unresolved access issues, retention exceptions and evidence reuse.

clauses.title: Measure knowledge by retrieval and decision use
clauses.text: Track whether evidence is findable, reused, cited in decisions, used to reduce duplicate research and trusted by researchers and decision makers.

clauses.title: Good knowledge systems make evidence reusable without making it unsafe
clauses.text: A mature data and knowledge system should increase the value of research evidence while reducing privacy risk, duplication, context loss and unsupported claims.

glossary.definition: An approved system or structured collection used to store, describe, retrieve and reuse research evidence.
The link between a research claim and the source material, method context, consent conditions and limitations that support it.
A documented rule that states what research material is retained, reviewed, archived or deleted and who approves exceptions.
The controlled use of existing research evidence to inform a new decision, reduce duplicate work or frame future research.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded DATA-STO-ACC into an end-to-end data and knowledge lifecycle covering operating model, evidence capture, metadata, access, retention, reuse and repository health.

pillars.title: Governance
pillars.operatingQuestion: What rules, review gates and escalation routes make research ethical, useful, lawful and consistent?
pillars.definition: The Governance pillar turns research principles into repeatable practice through review gates, ethics, safeguarding, privacy, quality standards and escalation paths.
pillars.valueFrame: Governance protects participants and the organisation while keeping research decisions clear, proportionate and usable.

sections.title: Governance operating model
sections.definition: How research governance is owned, scaled and made proportionate across the research lifecycle.

clauses.title: Governance is the operating system for responsible research
clauses.text: Research governance should turn ethical, legal, inclusion and quality principles into repeatable practice without removing accountable human judgement.

clauses.title: Name accountable governance roles
clauses.text: Every research practice must define who owns governance rules, who reviews studies, who approves exceptions and who can escalate unresolved risks.

clauses.title: Use proportionate review levels
clauses.text: Classify research as low, medium or high governance risk so routine work can move quickly and sensitive work receives specialist review.

clauses.title: Make governance usable
clauses.text: Governance reviewers must explain decisions in plain language, focus on material risk and help teams improve the work rather than only block it.

sections.title: Study initiation and risk triage
sections.definition: How studies enter governance review and how risk is assessed before participant contact.

clauses.title: Complete governance triage before participant contact
clauses.text: A study must complete governance triage before recruitment, screening, participant outreach or fieldwork begins.

clauses.title: Record the method and participant risk rationale
clauses.text: The study record must explain why the method, participant group, setting, incentives, recording and data handling approach are proportionate to the research question.

clauses.title: Escalate uncertainty early
clauses.text: Where risk is unclear, escalate before the plan hardens. Early review should reduce rework and prevent pressure to accept avoidable risk later.

sections.title: Ethics, consent and safeguarding
sections.definition: How teams protect participant agency, dignity, inclusion and safety.

clauses.title: Participant agency is a governance requirement
clauses.text: Participants should understand what they are being asked to do, how their information will be used, what choices they have and how to stop taking part.

clauses.title: Review high-risk ethics and safeguarding before approval
clauses.text: Research involving vulnerable participants, distressing topics, power imbalance, covert observation, minors, legal sensitivity or safeguarding concerns must receive specialist review before approval.

clauses.title: Do not trade participant welfare for delivery speed
clauses.text: People planning or approving research must not pressure teams to continue, recruit or publish where participant welfare risks are unresolved.

sections.title: Privacy, data protection and evidence governance
sections.definition: How participant data and research evidence are protected from collection through retention and reuse.

clauses.title: Define data handling before collection
clauses.text: The study must document what participant data will be collected, where it will be stored, who can access it, how long it will be retained and how deletion or withdrawal will be handled.

clauses.title: Protect direct identifiers from unnecessary reuse
clauses.text: Direct identifiers must not be included in research notes, findings, repositories or sharebacks unless the study has an approved and recorded reason.

clauses.title: Use summaries when raw evidence is not needed
clauses.text: Where a team needs learning rather than raw data, prefer governed summaries, excerpts or decision records that preserve context without widening access to sensitive material.

sections.title: Findings quality and decision assurance
sections.definition: How findings are checked before they influence decisions, policy, service design or delivery.

clauses.title: Review claims before findings are shared
clauses.text: Before findings are shared, claims must be checked against evidence, method limits, sample limits, consent constraints and the confidence the team can reasonably place in the work.

clauses.title: Recommendations must not outrun evidence
clauses.text: Research recommendations should be clearly separated from observations and should reflect the strength, relevance and limits of the evidence.

clauses.title: Ratify AI-assisted outputs before use
clauses.text: AI-assisted notes, summaries, tags or draft findings should be treated as working material until a researcher checks accuracy, context, consent limits and missing evidence.

sections.title: Incidents, exceptions and escalation
sections.definition: How governance issues are recorded, contained, escalated and resolved.

clauses.title: Record incidents and exceptions in one place
clauses.text: Participant harm concerns, consent problems, privacy issues, data loss, protocol deviations and approved exceptions must be recorded in a governed register.

clauses.title: Escalate unresolved material risk
clauses.text: Material risks that cannot be resolved by the study team must be escalated to the named governance owner before the research proceeds or findings are used.

clauses.title: Create a no-blame route for raising concerns
clauses.text: People involved in research must be able to raise governance concerns without being treated as obstructive or disloyal to delivery.

sections.title: Continuous governance and operating learning
sections.definition: How governance practice is reviewed, measured and improved over time.

clauses.title: Review governance patterns, not only individual studies
clauses.text: Governance reviews should look for repeated risks, unclear rules, slow approvals, recurring exceptions and parts of the operating model that need redesign.

clauses.title: Review sourcebook governance clauses on a fixed cycle
clauses.text: Governance clauses must have an owner and review cycle so rules remain current with law, organisational policy, technology, research practice and participant risk.

clauses.title: Good governance should increase trust and pace
clauses.text: A mature governance system should make safe research easier to run, help risky research get the right review and make the value of responsible practice visible.

glossary.definition: The first review that classifies a study by ethics, privacy, safeguarding, inclusion, method and operational risk.
A risk that could affect participant welfare, legal compliance, evidence quality, organisational trust or a significant service decision.
A documented decision to depart from a rule, with owner, rationale, controls, expiry and review route.
The checks that keep claims, limitations, participant protections and recommendations aligned before findings are used.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded GOVERN into an end-to-end governance lifecycle covering operating model, triage, ethics, safeguarding, privacy, findings assurance, incidents, exceptions and continuous improvement.

pillars.title: Tools and infrastructure
pillars.operatingQuestion: What tools, platforms, integrations and support processes make research reliable, secure and scalable?
pillars.definition: The Tools and infrastructure pillar covers research tooling, procurement, integration, support, access, automation, resilience and operational ownership.
pillars.valueFrame: Tools should reduce repeat effort, protect data and support the operating model instead of becoming unmanaged local workarounds.

sections.title: Tools and infrastructure operating model
sections.definition: How research tools, platforms, infrastructure, support and ownership fit the research operating system.

clauses.title: Infrastructure should make good practice easier
clauses.text: Research tools and infrastructure should make responsible research easier to run by reducing manual work, protecting participant data and supporting the agreed operating model.

clauses.title: Assign ownership before a research tool is used
clauses.text: Every research tool must have a named owner, approved use case, data handling position, access process, support route and lifecycle plan before it is used for live research.

clauses.title: Maintain a research tooling register
clauses.text: Use a tooling register to record each tool's owner, purpose, data types, user groups, integrations, suppliers, costs, assurance status, support model and review date.

clauses.title: Do not normalise unmanaged workarounds
clauses.text: People supporting research must not treat personal spreadsheets, private accounts, unapproved recording tools or unsupported scripts as acceptable infrastructure for live research.

sections.title: Tool evaluation, procurement and adoption
sections.definition: How tools are assessed, approved, introduced and adopted without creating hidden risk.

clauses.title: Evaluate tools before procurement or use
clauses.text: A research tool must be evaluated for user need, accessibility, privacy, security, data residency, integration, cost, support and exit before it is procured or used.

clauses.title: Tool choice should follow operating need
clauses.text: Tools should be selected because they support a defined research operation, not because a team prefers a feature, supplier or existing habit.

clauses.title: Pilot adoption with support and controls
clauses.text: When introducing a tool, pilot it with clear entry criteria, user support, feedback routes, risk controls and a decision point before wider rollout.

sections.title: Access, identity and permission management
sections.definition: How people receive, change and lose access to research tools and infrastructure.

clauses.title: Control access by role and research need
clauses.text: Access to research tools must be granted by role, project need, data sensitivity and training status, with admin access treated as exceptional.

clauses.title: Review access when teams or projects change
clauses.text: Review tool access when someone changes role, leaves a team, finishes a study, changes supplier status or no longer has a live research need.

clauses.title: Do not share credentials or participant data through informal channels
clauses.text: People must not share tool credentials, recordings, transcripts, participant contact details or exports through personal accounts, chat messages or unsupported storage.

sections.title: Integration, automation and workflow reliability
sections.definition: How research tools connect to workflows, data, repositories and automation safely.

clauses.title: Document integrations before live use
clauses.text: Integrations that move research data, participant records, consent states, repository entries or operational status must document source, destination, owner, permissions, failure mode and audit route.

clauses.title: Standardise before automating
clauses.text: Automation should be added to stable, understood and repeatable operations rather than used to hide unclear ownership, poor process design or missing governance.

clauses.title: Design for visible failure
clauses.text: Automated workflows should expose errors, retries, missing permissions and data mismatches so teams can recover without silent loss of research records.

sections.title: Support, service health and incident response
sections.definition: How tools are supported, monitored and recovered when they affect research delivery.

clauses.title: Define the support route for each critical tool
clauses.text: Critical research tools must have a support route that states who responds, what counts as urgent, how incidents are escalated and how users are updated.

clauses.title: Track service health in research terms
clauses.text: Monitor tool reliability by its effect on recruitment, sessions, consent, repository access, analysis, reporting and participant communications rather than uptime alone.

clauses.title: Tell affected teams when tooling risk changes
clauses.text: People responsible for tools must communicate material outages, security issues, permission changes or data problems to affected research teams quickly and plainly.

sections.title: Security, compliance and resilience
sections.definition: How tooling protects participants, research data, service continuity and organisational assurance.

clauses.title: Complete assurance for tools handling sensitive research material
clauses.text: Tools that handle sensitive participant data, recordings, transcripts, consent records or unreleased findings must complete security, privacy and compliance assurance before live use.

clauses.title: Plan continuity for live research moments
clauses.text: For tools used in recruitment, consent, sessions, incentives or participant communications, define manual fallback, recovery steps and participant-facing messages before disruption happens.

clauses.title: Resilience is part of participant care
clauses.text: Infrastructure resilience should protect participants from confusion, repeated requests, lost consent, missed payments and failed communications when tools break.

sections.title: Lifecycle review, retirement and improvement
sections.definition: How tools are reviewed, improved, consolidated or retired over time.

clauses.title: Review tool value and risk on a fixed cycle
clauses.text: Research tools must be reviewed on a fixed cycle for usage, cost, duplication, support burden, data risk, accessibility, user satisfaction and alignment with the operating model.

clauses.title: Plan for tool exit as part of adoption
clauses.text: When adopting a tool, document export options, retention effects, replacement triggers and what happens to stored research data if the tool is retired.

clauses.title: Good infrastructure makes responsible research easier to run
clauses.text: A mature tools and infrastructure system should make research more reliable, secure, inclusive and scalable while reducing unmanaged effort and local workaround risk.

glossary.definition: The person accountable for a research tool's approved use, access, support, assurance, lifecycle and escalation route.
A tool whose failure or misuse could materially affect recruitment, consent, participant care, data protection, evidence quality or research delivery.
The documented source, destination, data, owner, permissions, failure mode and audit route for a tool or workflow integration.
The plan for adopting, reviewing, supporting, changing and retiring a research tool or infrastructure component.

changeHistory.summary: Expanded INFRA-PROV into an end-to-end tools and infrastructure lifecycle covering operating model, tool evaluation, access, integration, support, security, resilience and retirement.
